event: Neolithic farmers settle the Rhine Valley region of present-day Liechtenstein, leaving early traces of agricultural habitation.
Bronze Age communities establish settlements on hillsides and the Eschnerberg, marking sustained human presence in the area.
Iron Age Hallstatt and later La Tène Celtic cultures occupy the region, with the Raetian peoples inhabiting the Alpine valleys.
The Roman Empire under Augustus conquers Raetia, incorporating the area into the empire and building a road along the Rhine Valley.
Alemannic Germanic tribes begin raiding and settling the region as Roman control over Raetia weakens.
Roman authority collapses in the area as the Western Empire disintegrates, leaving the Rhine Valley to Alemannic and Raeto-Romanic populations.
The region comes under Frankish rule as the Franks absorb the Alemannic territories of the upper Rhine.
Under the Carolingian Empire the area forms part of the county of Rhaetia, with churches and parishes established across the valley.
The Treaty of Verdun divides the Carolingian Empire, placing the region within East Francia, a precursor to the Holy Roman Empire.
The County of Vaduz is created as a separate fief, defining a territory that would later become the heart of Liechtenstein.
Vaduz is granted imperial immediacy, placing the county directly under the Holy Roman Emperor rather than any intermediate lord.
The Swabian War devastates the region as imperial and Swiss Confederation forces clash, causing destruction in the Rhine Valley.
The Lordship of Schellenberg and County of Vaduz pass through various noble families amid the upheavals of the late medieval period.
Count Kaspar von Hohenems acquires the territories, but his family's heavy debts and witch-hunt trials bring decline.
The Peace of Westphalia ends the Thirty Years' War, confirming the patchwork of imperial states including the small Vaduz lands.
Prince Johann Adam Andreas von Liechtenstein purchases the Lordship of Schellenberg from the indebted Hohenems family.
Prince Johann Adam Andreas buys the County of Vaduz, uniting both territories under the House of Liechtenstein.
Emperor Charles VI elevates Vaduz and Schellenberg into the Imperial Principality of Liechtenstein, creating the modern state.
Napoleon dissolves the Holy Roman Empire and Liechtenstein joins the Confederation of the Rhine, gaining full sovereignty.
The Congress of Vienna admits Liechtenstein to the German Confederation, confirming its independence among European states.
Prince Johann I grants Liechtenstein its first constitution, a limited charter establishing a small advisory assembly.
Prince Alois II becomes the first reigning prince to actually visit Liechtenstein, beginning closer ties between ruler and country.
Liechtenstein enters a customs union with the Austrian Empire, integrating its small economy with its larger neighbor.
Prince Johann II grants a new constitution creating an elected parliament (Landtag) with limited legislative powers.
The German Confederation dissolves after the Austro-Prussian War, and Liechtenstein becomes fully independent and disbands its army.
Liechtenstein formally abolishes its tiny standing army, declaring permanent disarmament and neutrality.
Liechtenstein remains neutral during World War I but suffers economic hardship due to its close ties with Austria-Hungary.
Following Austria-Hungary's collapse, Liechtenstein ends its customs union with Austria and turns toward Switzerland.
A new democratic constitution is adopted, establishing a constitutional monarchy with expanded parliamentary and popular rights.
Liechtenstein signs a customs and monetary union with Switzerland, adopting the Swiss franc and aligning its economy with Bern.
Prince Franz Joseph II becomes the first sovereign to take up permanent residence at Vaduz Castle, as Nazi Germany annexes neighboring Austria.
Liechtenstein maintains strict neutrality at the outbreak of World War II, avoiding occupation despite surrounding turmoil.
At war's end Liechtenstein grants asylum to around 500 soldiers of a Russian anti-Soviet army unit, refusing Soviet demands to repatriate them.
Liechtenstein joins the Council of Europe, marking its growing integration into international institutions.
Liechtenstein becomes one of the last countries in Europe to grant women the right to vote in national elections.
Prince Hans-Adam II accedes to the throne following the death of his father, Franz Joseph II.
Liechtenstein becomes a full member of the United Nations, gaining recognition as a sovereign state on the world stage.
Liechtenstein joins the European Free Trade Association (EFTA), strengthening its economic links with European partners.
Liechtenstein joins the European Economic Area (EEA) and the World Trade Organization, opening its market to the EU's single market.
International scrutiny over money laundering prompts Liechtenstein to begin major reforms of its secretive banking sector.
Voters approve a controversial constitutional referendum granting Prince Hans-Adam II expanded political powers, among the strongest of any European monarch.
Prince Hans-Adam II hands day-to-day governing duties to his son, Hereditary Prince Alois, while remaining head of state.
A tax data scandal erupts as stolen banking records expose foreign tax evasion through Liechtenstein, prompting transparency reforms.
Liechtenstein commits to OECD standards on tax cooperation, signing information-exchange agreements to shed its tax-haven image.
Voters reject a proposal to curb the prince's veto powers, reaffirming the monarchy's strong constitutional role.
Liechtenstein celebrates the 300th anniversary of its founding as a principality with nationwide festivities.
